coredata.dc:title: A dual-mode sensor for colorimetric and “turn-on” fluorescent detection of ascorbic acid
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yinghua
Wei, Zhenni
Duan, Wenxiu
Ren, Cuiling
Wu, Jiang
Liu, Dan
Chen, Hongli
coredata.dc:description: In the present work, a dual mode sensor, which was obtained by integrating B, N, S co-doped carbon dots and Fe3+ (BNS-CDs@Fe3+), was developed for sensing ascorbic acid (AA). The fluorescent signal of BNS-CDs@Fe3+ sensor could be enhanced by AA, and a good linear relationship was obtained in the range of 0.1–600 μM with a detection limit down to 0.05 μM. More fascinatingly, the color of the sensor solution gradually changed from blue to red with the increasing concentrations of AA, which allowed establishing a simple colorimetric assay for AA detection in the linear range of 1–110 μM with a detection limit of 0.3 μM. Notably, a paper sensor for the determination of AA was developed with a lowest visual concentration of 1 μM, providing a novel and convenient platform for AA detection. More importantly, cell imaging experiments showed that this sensor can be applied for AA detection in HeLa cell, suggesting this sensing system holds great potential in biosensing and bioimaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Colorimetric
“Turn–on” fluorescent
Red emission
l-ascorbic acid
Paper sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Possible mechanism of the BNS-CDs@Fe3+ sensor to AA in dual mode

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (no. 21207057 ) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities ( lzujbky-2016-43 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Influence of light illumination (A), ionic strength (B), temperature (C) and pH on the FL intensity of BNS-CDs@Fe3+.

(A) Fluorescence recovery of the BNS-CDs@Fe3+ sensor in the presence of different concentrations of AA (0, 0.1, 0.5, 10, 50, 100, 200, 400, 600, 700, 800, 1000 μM). (B) Linear plots of the FL intensity (λem = 595 nm) recovery of BNS-CDs@Fe3+ as a function of AA concentration, R2 = 0.9900, where F0 and F were FL intensity of BNS-CDs@Fe3+ in the absence and presence of AA, respectively. (C) Photographs of the solution of BNS-CDs@Fe3+ after adding different concentrations of AA under 365 nm UV lamp.

(A) UV–Vis absorbance of the BNS-CDs@Fe2+ sensor with the addition of 0–130 μM AA, inset was the photographs of the BNS-CDs@Fe2+ solutions in the absence and presence of 130 μM AA under sunlight. (B) The Linear plots of the absorbance corresponding to AA concentration, where A0 and A were the absorbance of BNS-CDs@Fe3+ sensor in the absence and presence of AA, respectively.
Photographs of the paper sensor with various concentrations of AA (0, 1, 10, 20, 50, 100 μM) under sunlight.

(A) FL intensity recovery (F/F0) of the BNS-CDs@Fe3+ sensor toward various interfering substance (a:AA, b:Thr, c:Try, d:L-Tyr, e:Lys, f:Arg, g:Glu, i:Gly, k:Ala, l:glucose, m:fructose, n:galactose, o:urea, p:Na+, q:Mg2+, r:K+, s:Ca2+: 6 mM; h:GSH, j:L-Cys, t:Cu2+, u:Hg2+, v:Fe2+, w:NO2 −, x:SO3 2−: 600 μM; black bars) and then separated addition of 600 μM AA (red bars), where F0 and F were FL intensity of BNS-CDs@Fe3+ in the absence and presence of AA or interfering substance, respectively. (B) photographs of the BNS-CDs@Fe3+ sensor with different interfering substances and AA under sunlight. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence microscopy images of Hela cells incubated with 300 μg mL−1 BNS-CDs and 500 μM FeCl3 for 6 h at 37 °C, and then incubated with 500 μM AA for 6 h at 37 °C (B). (C) and (D) were its corresponding bright-field transmission images, respectively.

(A) UV–Vis spectra of the BNS-CDs, BNS-CDs@Fe3+, and BNS-CDs@Fe3+ sensor with AA. (B) UV–Vis spectra of BNS-CDs@Fe3+ sensor with AA and different interfering substances (Thr, Try, L-Tyr, Lys, Arg, Glu, GSH, Gly, L-Cys, Ala). (C) FTIR spectra of the BNS-CDs@Fe3+ sensor before (black curve) and after (red curve) the addition of AA. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration of the sensing platform based on BNS-CDs@Fe3+ sensor for colorimetric-fluorescent dual mode analysis of AA.
Detection results of AA in urine and human serum samples.
Comparison of different probes for AA detection.

head.ce:title: A dual-mode sensor for colorimetric and “turn-on” fluorescent detection of ascorbic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, Key Laboratory of Nonferrous Metal Chemistry and Resources Utilization of Gansu Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry
Key Laboratory of Nonferrous Metal Chemistry and Resources Utilization of Gansu Province
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Sciences, University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei 230027, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Science and Technology of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present work, a dual mode sensor, which was obtained by integrating B, N, S co-doped carbon dots and Fe3+ (BNS-CDs@Fe3+), was developed for sensing ascorbic acid (AA). The fluorescent signal of BNS-CDs@Fe3+ sensor could be enhanced by AA, and a good linear relationship was obtained in the range of 0.1–600 μM with a detection limit down to 0.05 μM. More fascinatingly, the color of the sensor solution gradually changed from blue to red with the increasing concentrations of AA, which allowed establishing a simple colorimetric assay for AA detection in the linear range of 1–110 μM with a detection limit of 0.3 μM. Notably, a paper sensor for the determination of AA was developed with a lowest visual concentration of 1 μM, providing a novel and convenient platform for AA detection. More importantly, cell imaging experiments showed that this sensor can be applied for AA detection in HeLa cell, suggesting this sensing system holds great potential in biosensing and bioimaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Ascorbic acid (AA), as an enzyme cofactor, reducing agent, essential nutritional factor, and component of neurotransmitter related enzymes, plays a very crucial role in many processes in humans' daily life [1–3]. Meanwhile, serving as an antioxidant in beverages, food, animal feed, cosmetics and pharmaceutical formulations, it is also widely used in many fields [4,5]. Abnormality of AA in human body will cause a variety of diseases, such as scurvy, mental illness, and cancer [6]. Unfortunately, AA can only be obtained from food intake to maintain the necessary content for human body. Hence, the determination of AA is quite important in food safety and disease diagnosis.

Currently, a number of analytical methods including electrochemistry [7], colorimetry [8,9], fluorometry [10–13], chemiluminescence [14], enzymology [15] and capillary electrophoresis [16] have been developed for AA determination. Among these methods, fluorescent and colorimetric detecting techniques were widely used. For example, Zhu et al. prepared photoluminescent nitrogen-doped carbon nanoparticles for monitoring AA in human biological fluids [3]. Liu et al. established a fluorescence sensing system based graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-Cu2+ for sensitive detection of AA [11]. Wang's group reported a probe based on gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) and single stranded DNA (ssDNA) for colorimetric sensing AA [17]. Fan et al. designed Co3O4 nanoparticles-decorated crumpled graphene microsphere for colorimetric detecting AA [18]. As expected, AA could be sensitively and effectively monitored through fluorescent method, and could be rapidly and conveniently monitored by colorimetric method. If the fluorescent and colorimetric methods were taken together, it will provide a more effective, convenient and sensitive method for the detection of AA.

Recently, colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode sensors have attracted much attention because they not only provide a highly sensitive fluorescence assay, but also facilitate the easy visualization of the target by bare eyes [19–24]. For instance, a novel colorimetric-fluorescent dual mode probe has been synthesized for the effective determination of thiols, and it was successfully applied to image biothiols in Hela cells [25]. Xu et al. developed a novel probe based metal organic frameworks encapsulating carbon dots (CDs) and Eu3+ for the colorimetric and fluorescent detection of Hg2+, and this novel fluorescent probe was used for ratiometric sensing Hg2+ with a detection limit of 13 ppb [26]. However, very little information is available for colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode sensing AA.

Hence, in this paper, we found that the fluorescent (FL) intensity of the ensemble of Fe3+ and red emissive B, N, S co-doped carbon dots (BNS-CDs) can be recovered by AA and the change of the solution color can also be observed by naked eyes, so a colorimetric and “turn-on” fluorescent dual-mode assay for the detection of AA was developed (Scheme 1 ). This sensor has three major advantages. First of all, different from the reported colorimetric-fluorescent dual mode sensors, this dual mode sensor was just based on CDs and Fe3+, implying its simplicity. And then, compared with the reported “turn-off” fluorescent pattern, this sensor can detect AA by “turn-on” fashion, which exhibited higher accuracy because it can avoid false results [27,28]. Finally, test paper for the detection of AA could be designed based on this sensor. More importantly, this sensor can be successfully applied for monitoring intracellular AA, possessing more potential in biological fields.

2, 5-diaminobenzenesulfonic acid was bought from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 4-aminophenylboronic acid hydrochloride was purchased from Soochiral Chemistry Science & Technology Co., Ltd. (Suzhou, China). Tris(hydroxymthyl)aminomethane (Tris), Hydrochloric acid (HCl), FeCl3, NaCl, MgCl2, KCl, CaCl2, NaNO2, Na2SO3, CuCl2, HgCl2, FeCl2, ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) and all amino acids (Thr, Try, L-Tyr, Lys, Arg, Glu, GSH, Gly, L-Cys, Ala) were acquired from Beijing Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Beijing, China). Glucose, fructose, galactose and urea were purchased from Xi'an Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Xi'an, China). All reagents were of analytical grade and used without any further purification. All stock and buffer solutions were prepared using deionized (DI) water throughout the experiments. A 1.0 kDa molecular weight cutoff membrane (Amicon Ultra-4, Millipore) was used. The human urine and serum samples were provided by the first affiliated hospital of Lanzhou University and the authors state that all experiments were performed in compliance with the relevant laws and institutional guidelines.

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was recorded on a Nicolet Nexus 670 FTIR spectrometer (Nicolet Co., Ltd., America) with KBr pellets mixed with freeze-drying sample power. UV–visible absorption spectra were measured by a TU-1901 double beam UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co., Ltd., Beijing, China). Fluorescent spectra were measured on a RF-5301 spectrofluorophotometer (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan) equipped with a Xe lamp using 5/5 nm slit width, and equipped with a 1 cm quartz cell. The quantum yield was measured using a time-resolved and steady state fluorescence spectrometer FLS 920 (Edinburgh). Zeta potentials of the samples were measured by a Zetasizer Nano ZS instrument (Malvern, UK). Photos under sunlight and UV lamp were obtained by a camera (Nikon). Cellular imaging was carried out using a spinning-disk confocal microscope (Nikon's inverted Eclipse Ti microscope).

The BNS-CDs@Fe3+ sensor was developed by simply mixing BNS-CDs and Fe3+ together. BNS-CDs were prepared according to our previous reported method [29]. Typically, 0.075 g 2, 5-diaminobenzenesulfonic acid and 0.075 g 4-aminophenylboronic acid hydrochloride were dissolved in 30 mL DI water, and then the solution was transferred into a Teflon autoclave. After being heated at 180 °C for 8 h, the autoclave was cooled down to room temperature naturally. The obtained red suspension was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 15 min to remove large particles. And the supernatant was dialysis (1000 Da) for 12 h. Finally, 10 μL of Fe3+ (500 μM) was added into the mixture of BNS-CDs (5 mg/mL) and Tris-HCl buffer (10 mM, pH = 7.0) solution, and then the BNS-CDs@Fe3+ sensor was obtained.

The fluorescent detection of AA was conducted as followed: 500 μL of the BNS-CDs was mixed with 2.5 mL Tris-HCl buffer solution (10 mM, pH = 7.0), then 10 μL Fe3+ (500 μM) solution was added, subsequently different concentrations of AA (10 μL each) were separated added into the BNS-CDs@Fe3+ sensor solution. The fluorescent spectra (λex = 500 nm) were recorded after 1 min. The selectivity for AA determination was evaluated by adding other interfering substances instead of AA in a similar way. The anti-interference experiment was performed by adding AA and interfering substances simultaneously in a similar way. All experiments were performed at room temperature. Human urine and serum were used to explore the feasibility of the detecting AA in practical samples. Standard addition method was used to evaluate the applicability of the BNS-CDs@Fe3+ sensor. The urine and serum samples were subjected to a 100-fold dilution before analysis, and then the diluted samples were spiked with various concentrations of standard AA solution for the determining AA.

For the colorimetric detection of AA, 50 μL of the BNS-CDs was mixed with 2.95 mL Tris-HCl buffer solution (10 mM, pH = 7.0), then 10 μL Fe3+ (500 μM) solution was added, subsequently different concentrations of AA (10 μL each) solution were separated added into the BNS-CDs@Fe3+ sensor. The absorbance at 645 nm was then recorded using UV–Vis spectrophotometry.

The stability for an objective sensor is an important feature in practical applications. Therefore, the influences of light exposure, ion strength, temperature and pH on the FL intensity of the BNS-CDs@Fe3+ sensor were explored. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the FL intensity of the sensor decreased slightly after 60 min of light illumination, certified its good photostability. In addition, the FL intensity of this sensor remained almost unchanged even in 0.1 M NaCl solution (Fig. 1B) or in the temperature range of 20–45 °C (Fig. 1C). But the FL intensity this sensor was decreased in the pH range of 3.0–9.0, as displayed in Fig. 1D. Its sensitivity to pH may originate from protonation-deprotonation [30]. In consideration of the recovery efficiency and physiological applications, pH 7.0 was chosen as the optimal pH in the following experiments.

In this study, the fluorescence of BNS-CDs@Fe3+ could be recovered by AA along with the quantum yield changed from 0.7% to 2.3% [29]. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the FL intensity of this sensor can be gradually enhanced with increasing concentrations of AA, and there was a good linear relationship (Fig. 2B) between the FL intensity and the concentration of AA in the range of 0.1–600 μM (R2 = 0.9900), and the limit of detection is calculated to be 0.05 μM (3s/k, in which s was the standard deviation for the blank solution, and k was the slope of the calibration curve). As for an effective sensor, the reversibility is greatly required for practical applications. The reversibility experiments were explored via recording the fluorescent spectra changes, as shown in Fig. S1, the fluorescent intensity of sensor displayed negligible decrease even after five cycles, demonstrating the superior reversibility. Meanwhile, the gradually recovered red fluorescence of BNS-CDs@Fe3+ can be seen under UV lamp (Fig. 2C). Furthermore, the detection process can be finished in 1 min (Fig. S2), implying this sensor could be used as an efficient probe for real time sensing AA in biological system.

Interestingly, upon the addition of AA, the color of the solution changed from blue to red, which can be clearly monitored by naked eyes (Fig. 3 A inset). At the same time, as illustrated in Fig. 3A, a concomitant decrease of the absorbance at 645 nm was observed. As expected, a good linear correlation between the absorbance and the concentrations of AA can be obtained in the concentration range of 1–110 μM (Fig. 3B). The limit of detection was calculated to be 0.3 μM (3s/k, in which s was the standard deviation for the blank solution, and k was the slope of the calibration curve). Considering the practicability, a paper sensor for convenient and rapid determination of AA was developed. As displayed in Fig. 4 , with the concentrations of AA increasing, the color of the test paper gradually changed from blue to light pink. Significantly, the color of the test paper at 1 μM AA was lighter than that at 0 μM AA, indicating the lowest visual concentration of AA was 1 μM. Thus, it was clearly demonstrated that it was applicable for constructing a test paper towards AA.

As the physiological environment is complex, the selectivity of the sensor towards AA is as important as the sensitivity. For investigating the selectivity of the sensor toward AA, the effects of some representative interfering substances potentially coexisted in urine and serum on determining AA have been investigated, including various amino acid, metal cations and anions (Thr, Try, L-Tyr, Lys, Arg, Glu, GSH, Gly, L-Cys, Ala, glucose, fructose, galactose, urea, Na+, Mg2+, K+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Fe2+, NO2 −, SO3 2−). As shown in Fig. 5 A, the black bars represented the FL response of the BNS-CDs@Fe3+ sensor towards the interfering substances. While, the red bars represented the subsequent addition of 600 μM AA to the sensor. As might be expected, only L-Cys and GSH exhibited slight interference for fluorescent detecting AA. Moreover, Fig. 5B indicating dramatic color change (from blue to red) can be observed only in the present of AA. All the above results demonstrated AA can be selectively detected via colorimetric and fluorescent dual mode.

Before cell imaging, the cytotoxicity of the BNS-CDs@Fe3+ sensor toward HeLa cell was measured by the MTT assay (Fig. S3). The result displayed that even incubated with 300 μg/mL BNS-CDs@Fe3+ for 24 h, the cell viability was still over 80%. The applicability of the sensor in intracellular imaging of AA was shown in Fig. 7, after incubating Hela cells with BNS-CDs@Fe3+ at 37 °C for 6 h, a slight red emission from the intracellular region could be observed (Fig. 6 A). The red signal from the intracellular region can be enhanced after subsequent incubation with 500 μM AA at 37 °C for 6 h (Fig. 6B), indicated this sensor could be applied for detecting intracellular AA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of the BNS-CDs@Fe3+ sensor to AA in dual mode
ce:section.ce:para: Sensing mechanism of this sensor for AA was further explored by UV–Vis spectra, FTIR spectra and zeta potential measurements. As shown in Fig. 7 A and B, the absorption peak of the BNS-CDs@Fe3+ centred at 645 nm, which resulting from the coordination of -NH2 on the surface of BNS-CDs with Fe3+ [29], can be eliminated only by AA. NO2 − and SO3 2−, which also have reduction capability, can not make the fluorescent signal of BNS-CDs@Fe3+ be recovered and change the solution color (Fig. 5A and B), implied the detection mechanism was not due to redox effects. In our previous work, the interaction of Fe3+ with the −COOH, −OH and nitrogen functional groups on the BNS-CDs surfaces can quench the fluorescent emission of BNS-CDs. So we deduced the interaction between the −OH of AA and Fe3+ made contributed to the dual mode response of BNS-CDs@Fe3+ towards AA. In order to prove this hypothesis, FL and UV–Vis spectra measurements of BNS-CDs@ Fe3+ with addition of EDTA were carried out. After adding EDTA, it could be seen the emission intensity at 595 nm was greatly enhanced and the absorbance at 645 nm was decreased (Fig. S4), preliminarily proved our hypothesis was correct. Subsequently, zeta potential of the BNS-CDs@Fe3+ in the presents of AA was measured. As displayed in Table S1, the potential value was changed from −10.80 to −8.52 mv after the addition of AA, which was more positive than that of BNS-CDs@Fe3+. According to our previous work [29], the coordination of Fe3+ with the amino groups of BNS-CDs can shield part of positive charge of BNS-CDs, so the change of the surface charge after the addition of AA proved AA can interact with Fe3+ and release these amino groups. In order to further prove this mechanism, FTIR spectra of BNS-CDs@Fe3+ before and after the addition of AA was measured. As displayed in Fig. 7C, after the addition of AA, there was a new peak at 1649 cm−1 which was attributed to the N−H bending vibrations [31,32], proving the interaction of –OH of AA with Fe3+ can make these amino groups released. Based on these results, the dual mode detection mechanism of this sensor for AA was due to the complexation between Fe3+ and –OH of AA.

In summary, a colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode strategy for AA sensing was established based on BNS-CDs@Fe3+ sensor and the detection mechanism was also discussed. This sensor showed good water-solubility and remarkable stability, it can not only realize detecting AA by “turn-on” fluorescent mode, but also detect AA colorimetrically, so a test paper was developed for determining AA based on this sensor. Furthermore, this sensor offered excellent performance towards AA detection in urine and human serum and it can also be applied for monitoring intracellular AA.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (no. 21207057) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (lzujbky-2016-43).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2017.10.039.
